text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)
PRE-OPERATIVE RESTAGING — POST-NEOADJUVANT FOLFIRINOX (8 CYCLES)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with a known history of borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0 at initial staging), KRAS G12V / TP53 / SMAD4-mutated, microsatellite stable. He has completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy (with oxaliplatin dose reduction to 68 mg/m2 beginning cycle 5 for cumulative peripheral neuropathy, and irinotecan dose reduction to 135 mg/m2 following cycle 7). A biliary plastic stent was placed at diagnosis for biliary decompression. Interim restaging after cycle 4 demonstrated partial response with tumor reduction from 3.8 cm to 3.1 cm and improvement in superior mesenteric artery abutment. Present study is obtained for formal end-of-neoadjuvant restaging to guide surgical planning. Serum CA 19-9 at time of scan: 85 U/mL (baseline 1200 U/mL at diagnosis; 380 U/mL at mid-treatment restaging).

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed on a 64-slice scanner with intravenous iohexol contrast using a pancreatic protocol triphasic acquisition: non-contrast, early arterial (pancreatic parenchymal phase, approximately 40 seconds), late arterial/portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds), and delayed phase (180 seconds). Multiplanar reconstructions performed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Oral contrast was not administered. Comparison: prior CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (mid-treatment restaging, approximately 8 weeks prior) and original diagnostic staging CT.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a persistent hypovascular, ill-defined mass centered in the head of the pancreas, now measuring approximately 2.4 cm in greatest axial dimension on the portal venous phase (previously 3.1 cm on the mid-treatment restaging study and 3.8 cm at the time of initial diagnosis). The lesion demonstrates continued reduction in size compared with both prior examinations, representing an overall decrease of approximately 1.4 cm from baseline. There is increased internal heterogeneity with areas of low-attenuation centrally within the mass, which may reflect evolving treatment-related necrosis, fibrosis, or stromal remodeling in keeping with chemotherapeutic response. No cystic degeneration or new calcification is identified within the lesion. Peripancreatic soft tissue infiltration and upstream pancreatic ductal dilation persist but appear somewhat diminished compared with baseline; the main pancreatic duct upstream of the mass measures approximately 4 mm (previously 6 mm at diagnosis), consistent with partial decompression. The body and tail of the pancreas appear atrophic, consistent with obstructive changes of the duct. The pancreatic parenchyma enhances within normal limits in the uninvolved portions.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:

Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The SMA is visualized along its entire course from the aortic origin. On both the arterial and portal venous phase, the fat plane between the tumor and the right lateral wall of the SMA appears substantially improved relative to the baseline study. A thin rim of residual soft tissue contact is present along less than 90 degrees of the arterial circumference — markedly improved from the 120–150 degrees of abutment documented at initial staging. The SMA adventitia appears intact without deformity or narrowing of the arterial lumen. No perivascular tumor cuffing is identified. This configuration would be considered borderline/limited abutment at most, representing meaningful improvement from prior staging.

Superior Mesenteric Vein / Portal Vein (SMV/PV) Confluence: The SMV-portal vein confluence is patent. Previously documented short-segment contact of the tumor with the SMV is improved on the current study; the fat plane along the SMV appears at least partially restored in some regions. There is no definite luminal narrowing, deformity, or thrombosis of the SMV or main portal vein. The interface between the posterior-medial soft tissue and the SMV remains close but does not demonstrate clear circumferential encasement. The portal vein proper and splenic vein appear widely patent.

Celiac Axis: The celiac axis arises normally from the aorta. There is no contact of the tumor mass with the celiac trunk or its major branches (common hepatic artery, splenic artery). The hepatic artery proper is patent and without encasement.

HEPATIC PARENCHYMA:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without discrete focal lesions. No hypodense, hypervascular, or ring-enhancing hepatic masses are identified across all three phases of enhancement. No capsular contour abnormality or subcapsular fluid. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent. No biliary ductal dilation within the hepatic parenchyma. A small simple hepatic cyst (< 5 mm) is noted in segment VI, unchanged and of no clinical significance.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
A plastic biliary stent is again noted in situ within the distal common bile duct/intrapancreatic segment, extending into the duodenum. Mild periductal thickening is present in the intrapancreatic segment of the CBD, likely related to the adjacent tumor and/or stent. There is no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The common hepatic duct measures approximately 5 mm in caliber (within normal limits above the level of the stent).

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is present, non-distended, and without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or cholelithiasis. No biloma.

SPLEEN AND LEFT UPPER QUADRANT:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation without focal lesions. The splenic vein is patent, and no splenic vein thrombosis is identified.

ADREND GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology and attenuation. No adrenal mass.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal parenchymal lesion. No hydronephrosis or perinephric stranding. No renal calculi.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously noted periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament lymph nodes have continued to diminish in size. The largest periportal node now measures approximately 0.6 cm in short axis (previously 0.8 cm at mid-treatment; 1.2 cm at baseline staging), falling within normal size limits. No discrete rounded, necrotic, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, para-aortic, aortocaval, or pelvic nodal stations. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants or soft tissue nodularity suspicious for carcinomatosis. No ascites. No omental thickening or infiltration. The small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness without obstruction.

PELVIS:
No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid. Urinary bladder partially distended and unremarkable. Prostate measures within normal limits for age without discrete nodule.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions within the imaged skeleton. No soft tissue masses.

IMPRESSION:

1. CONTINUED RESPONSE TO NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY: The pancreatic head mass has further decreased to approximately 2.4 cm in greatest axial dimension, down from 3.1 cm at mid-treatment restaging and 3.8 cm at initial diagnosis — representing a substantial and progressive reduction in tumor bulk over the course of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX.

2. MARKEDLY IMPROVED VASCULAR ANATOMY: The degree of abutment of the superior mesenteric artery has continued to diminish, now estimated at less than 90 degrees of circumference with partial restoration of the perivascular fat plane. This represents a notable improvement from the 120–150 degrees of abutment documented at baseline. The SMV-portal vein confluence remains patent without evidence of definite encasement, also reflecting improvement from the initial short-segment involvement.

3. RESOLUTION OF SUSPICIOUS LYMPHADENOPATHY: Previously enlarged and suspicious periportal lymph nodes have regressed to within normal size limits, measuring 0.6 cm at the largest (previously up to 1.2 cm at diagnosis).

4. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No hepatic lesions, no peritoneal carcinomatosis, no ascites, no distant lymphadenopathy, and no osseous metastases are identified.

5. These findings, taken in conjunction with the marked decline in serum CA 19-9 (85 U/mL, down from 1200 U/mL at baseline), are consistent with a meaningful biochemical and radiographic response to neoadjuvant therapy. The improved vascular relationships raise the possibility that surgical resection may now be technically feasible and warrant multidisciplinary team evaluation for potential pancreaticoduodenectomy.

Biliary stent in situ without complication.
